---
title: "Testing concepts"
subtitle: "Testing training — Module 02"
lang: en
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    code-block-height: 400px
---

## 🇫🇷

[🇫🇷 Version française](../../slides/02_testing_concepts.qmd)

---

## Anatomy of a test

```
GIVEN  a TSV file with 20 rows                ← Arrange
WHEN   I run validate_records.sh              ← Act
THEN   the exit code is 0                     ← Assert
AND    the output says "20 records validated"
```

GIVEN / WHEN / THEN = Arrange / Act / Assert — same concept, two notations.

. . .

The test name **is** the specification: `test_filter_excludes_values_below_threshold` says what the code must do — before even reading it.

::: {.notes}
GIVEN/WHEN/THEN comes from BDD (Behaviour-Driven Development). AAA comes from the xUnit tradition. Both describe the exact same structure — just two different vocabularies used by different communities. This course uses GIVEN/WHEN/THEN because it reads more naturally aloud.

On test names: this is often underestimated. A test named `test_filter` says nothing. `test_filter_excludes_values_below_threshold` states what the code guarantees. If someone breaks that behaviour, the failing test name is enough to understand what went wrong — without reading the code.

Useful question for the group: "If your test fails at 3am in CI, does the test name alone tell you what broke?"
:::

---

## The test pyramid {.smaller}

```
        ▲  Functional    — full pipeline, slow, few
       ▲▲  Integration   — chained modules, medium speed
      ▲▲▲  Unit          — functions, isolated processes, fast, many
```

| Type | Scope | In nf-test |
|------|-------|------------|
| **Unit** | one function, one process | `nextflow_process` |
| **Integration** | several chained modules | `nextflow_workflow` |
| **Functional** | full pipeline | `nextflow_pipeline` |
| **Non-regression** | before/after comparison | snapshots |

::: {style="font-size:0.55em; color:#666; margin-top:0.5em;"}
→ [TestPyramid — Martin Fowler](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/TestPyramid.html)
:::

::: {.notes}
The pyramid is not a strict rule — it's a proportion guide. Many fast unit tests, few slow functional tests. If the pyramid is inverted (more slow tests than fast ones), CI becomes slow and developers start ignoring results.

In bioinformatics, the situation is particular: a "unit test" for a FASTQC process reads files, calls an executable, and touches the filesystem. That's not a pure unit test in the strict software engineering sense — Google would call it a Medium test. But in our context, we call it a unit test because it tests a single isolated component.

If someone asks "how many tests of each type?", the honest answer is: as many unit tests as needed to cover the business rules, a few integration tests for critical chains, one or two functional tests for the main paths. No magic formula.
:::

---

## Terms vary across communities {.smaller}

| This deck | Other common names |
|---------|---------------------|
| Functional | End-to-end, acceptance test, e2e, system test |
| Non-regression | Regression test, change detection |
| Unit | Component test (some JS frameworks) |
| — | Smoke test: minimal test, checks it starts |

. . .

In bioinformatics: a "unit test" for a FASTQC module reads files and calls an executable.

→ Google calls it a **Medium test**, not a unit test — because it touches the filesystem.

. . .

**In this course: Unit / Integration / Functional / Non-regression.**

::: {style="font-size:0.55em; color:#666; margin-top:0.5em;"}
→ [TestPyramid](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/TestPyramid.html) · [Google Test Sizes](https://testing.googleblog.com/2010/12/test-sizes.html) · [ISTQB Glossary](https://glossary.istqb.org/en_US/term)
:::

::: {.notes}
This slide exists to defuse terminology debates. If someone comes from a JavaScript context and says "but that's not a unit test", you can say: "You're right by the strict definition, but in this course we use 'unit' to mean 'one isolated component'."

Smoke test deserves a mention: it's the test that just checks the program starts and doesn't immediately crash. Often the first test written. Useful but not sufficient on its own.

Don't spend more than 2–3 minutes on this slide. The goal is to fix vocabulary for the rest of the course, not to survey every existing taxonomy.
:::

---

## Assertions {.smaller}

An assertion = a binary check: true → test passes, false → test fails.

| What we check | Example |
|--------------|---------|
| File exists | `assert path.exists()` |
| Number of rows | `assert len(df) == 20` |
| Expected columns | `assert "id" in df.columns` |
| Value within bounds | `assert (df["value"] >= 0).all()` |
| Exit code | `assert returncode == 0` |

. . .

```python
assert result is not None        # too vague — detects nothing precise
assert len(result) == 10         # exact — detects data loss
assert result["A"] == 65.25      # business value — detects a wrong calculation
```

A useful assertion detects **precisely** what can be wrong.

::: {.notes}
`assert result is not None` is the most common antipattern. It passes if the function returns anything at all — an empty list, an empty DataFrame, a wrong dictionary. It protects almost nothing.

The question to ask for every assertion: "If this test fails, what does it tell me?" If the answer is "not much", the assertion is too vague.

`assert result["A"] == 65.25` is a good business assertion: it checks a specific calculation. If the module 01 bug (filter applied with the wrong threshold) were present, this assertion would catch it. `assert result is not None` would not.

Don't go into `pytest.approx` for floats here — that's covered in module 03.
:::

---

## Test isolation

Each test must be able to run on its own, in any order.

```python
# Bad — shared side effect between two real tests
def test_load_file():
    df = load_records("records.tsv")
    df.to_csv("/tmp/out.tsv", sep="\t")    # side effect
    assert len(df) == 20

def test_positive_values():
    df = pd.read_csv("/tmp/out.tsv", sep="\t")  # depends on the previous test
    assert (df["value"] >= 0).all()
```

. . .

```python
# Correct — each test arranges its own data
def test_positive_values():
    df = load_records("records.tsv")       # no external dependency
    assert (df["value"] >= 0).all()
```

::: {.notes}
The problem with the "bad" example: if `test_load_file` doesn't run first (or if pytest runs them in a different order), `test_positive_values` fails — but the cause is invisible. In parallel CI, this type of bug is hard to reproduce.

`/tmp` is a common antipattern: the file may already exist from a previous run, may be modified by another test running in parallel, or may not exist at all depending on execution order.

In pytest, the correct solution is to use the `tmp_path` fixture, which creates a unique temporary directory per test. This is covered in module 03.

Useful question: "Has anyone had a test that sometimes passed and sometimes failed with no apparent reason?" That's almost always an isolation problem.
:::

---

## Parameterised tests

**Happy path** (nominal input) + **edge cases** (limits, invalid) — without duplicating code.

```python
def test_valid_records():              # happy path — 1 nominal case
    df = load_records(DATA / "records.tsv")
    assert len(df) == 20

@pytest.mark.parametrize("path,exc", [  # edge cases — without duplicating
    ("empty.tsv",   ValueError),
    ("missing.tsv", FileNotFoundError),
])
def test_invalid_inputs(path, exc):
    with pytest.raises(exc):
        load_records(DATA / path)
```

. . .

```python
# ❌ loop inside a test — stops at first failure, opaque name
def test_edge_cases():
    for path in ["empty.tsv", "missing.tsv"]:
        with pytest.raises(...): load_records(path)
```

::: {.notes}
The critical difference between `parametrize` and a loop inside a test: with `parametrize`, each case is an independent test with its own name. If `empty.tsv` fails but `missing.tsv` passes, you see it clearly. With a loop, the test stops at the first failure — you don't know if the other cases pass or not.

In practice, `parametrize` generates automatic names like `test_invalid_inputs[empty.tsv-ValueError]`. Readable in the test report.

The happy path should always exist separately. Parametrised tests are for variations of the same behaviour — not for mixing the nominal case with error cases in the same test.
:::

---

## Snapshots

A snapshot = a **photograph** of the expected output. Available in all frameworks.

```r
expect_snapshot(compute_stats(df))   # testthat
```
```groovy
assert snapshot(process.out).match() // nf-test
```
```bash
diff <(bash validate_records.sh records.tsv) expected.txt  # bats
```

First run → creates the reference. Subsequent runs → compares — difference → failure.

::: {.callout-warning}
A snapshot does not say the output is **correct**. It says it has **not changed**.

Snapshot alone = non-regression safety net. Snapshot + explicit assertion = useful test.
:::

::: {.notes}
The most common snapshot mistake: updating them automatically without checking why they changed. A snapshot change may be intentional (you changed the behaviour) or reveal a bug. If you blindly accept the new snapshot, the bug is now the "new reference".

Correct workflow: snapshot fails → understand why → if intentional change, update → if bug, fix the code.

In nf-test, `--update-snapshot` regenerates snapshots. Only use it after verifying that the change is expected.

Good analogy: a snapshot is like a passport photo. If the photo changes, either someone aged (normal) or someone falsified the document (bug). Check before accepting.
:::

---

## Test data

**Rule: the minimum size that triggers the behaviour being tested.**

```
data/
├── records.tsv    # 20 rows (id, group, value)
└── empty.tsv      # header only, no data
```

20 rows are enough for a filter or a stats calculation.
100 reads are enough for a FASTQC process.

::: {.notes}
The temptation is to use "realistic" data — thousands of rows, real FASTQs. Two problems: tests become slow, and when a test fails it's hard to understand why on a large file.

20 rows for `records.tsv` is not arbitrary: it's enough to have two groups (A and B), values to filter, stats to compute — but small enough to check manually if needed.

The error files (`empty.tsv`, `invalid_records.tsv`, etc.) each violate a single rule. This is intentional: one file = one violated rule = one diagnostic test. If multiple rules are violated in one file, you don't know which one caused the failure.

Never put real biological data in `training/data/` — confidentiality, size, and reproducibility issues.
:::

---

## Coverage: 5 tests, 100 % — and 3 bugs

```bash
$ pytest --cov=filter_and_summarise test_coverage_demo.py

Name                         Stmts   Miss  Cover
-------------------------------------------------
filter_and_summarise.py         14      0   100%

5 passed in 0.4s
```

. . .

::: {.callout-warning}
A silent bug does not raise an exception — it produces a slightly wrong result.

Coverage measures whether lines are **executed**, not whether assertions **prove anything**.
:::

::: {.notes}
This slide is the main argument against "we have 100% coverage, we're covered". Coverage measures which lines were executed — not whether the assertions checked anything useful.

The file `test_coverage_demo.py` is in the repo — a live demo is possible if time allows: run the tests, see 100%, then show the 3 bugs on the next slide.

The 3 bugs are: (1) `>` instead of `>=` in the filter — S02 with value=45 is silently excluded; (2) stats computed on the full DataFrame instead of the filtered one; (3) the function returns a Series instead of a DataFrame. All 5 tests still pass.

Question to ask: "What coverage percentage do you aim for in your current projects?" If someone says "100%", this is the moment to explain that it's neither achievable nor desirable as an absolute target.
:::

---

## What coverage does not see

```python
# Bug 1 — > instead of >= : S02 (value=45) silently excluded
assert len(filtered) == 10              # ← exact count
assert 45 in filtered["value"].values  # ← boundary invariant

# Bug 2 — stats computed on df, not on filtered
assert result["A"] == pytest.approx(65.25)  # ← business value

# Bug 3 — returns Series, not DataFrame
assert isinstance(result, pd.DataFrame)     # ← explicit type
```

→ Coverage tells you what you have **not tested**. Not whether what you have tested is **correct**.

::: {.notes}
Walk through each correction line by line, explaining what it catches.

Bug 1: `assert len(filtered) == 10` catches over- or under-filtering. `assert 45 in filtered["value"].values` is the boundary invariant — strict equality (`>=`) must include the threshold value.

Bug 2: `pytest.approx` is needed for floats — don't use `== 65.25` directly. The value 65.25 is the correct mean on the filtered data.

Bug 3: the type is often forgotten. A function can return a pandas Series instead of a DataFrame — no exception, but code downstream may fail in unexpected ways.

Emphasise the final message: coverage is a useful tool for finding untested areas, but high coverage does not guarantee that the tests are good.
:::

---

## Next → Module 03

Before opening module 03: here is how GIVEN/WHEN/THEN translates to bats.

```bash
# GIVEN  valid records.tsv        →  DATA="training/data"
# WHEN   validate_records.sh      →  run bash validate_records.sh "$DATA/records.tsv"
# THEN   exit 0                   →  [ "$status" -eq 0 ]
# THEN   message with count       →  [[ "$output" =~ "20 records" ]]
```

Module 03 shows the three syntaxes — bats, pytest, testthat — on this same example.

::: {.notes}
This slide bridges the abstract concepts of module 02 with the concrete practice of module 03. The GIVEN/WHEN/THEN structure maps directly to bats syntax.

`run` is the central keyword in bats: it executes the command and captures `$status` (exit code) and `$output` (standard output). Without `run`, those variables are not available.

Resist explaining the full bats syntax here — module 03 does that. This slide is only the transition.

If the group looks tired, this is a good moment for a 5-minute break before starting module 03.
:::
